coredata.dc:title: Controlled delivery of sildenafil by β-Cyclodextrin-decorated sulfur-doped carbon nanodots: a synergistic activation of ROS signaling in tumors overexpressing PDE-5
coredata.dc:creator: Mauro, Nicolò
Cillari, Roberta
Andrea Utzeri, Mara
Costa, Salvatore
Giammona, Gaetano
Nicosia, Aldo
Cavallaro, Gennara
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent sulfur- and nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (CDs) are zero-dimensional nanoparticles that mediate ROS production in cancer cells, displaying inherent anticancer properties. Thus, they have been proposed as nanotheranostic tools useful in image-guided cancer therapy. Here, we try to show that cancerous cells (high PDE-5 expression) receiving sildenafil delivered by CDs-based nanostructures promote positive reinforcement of PDE-5-mediated cell death via the overexpression of genes involved in the production of ROS. We explored the regioselective Huisgen cycloaddition between azide-β-cyclodextrin and CDs-alkyne to synthetize homogeneous nanostructures, named CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx, consisting of CDs functionalized at the surface with β-cyclodextrins capable of including high amount drugs such as sildenafil (>20 % w/w), and releasing them in a controlled manner. We investigated how CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx bearing sildenafil enter cells, enhancing ROS production and cell death specifically in cancer cells overexpressing PDE-5. These nanoplatforms go beyond the bounds of EPR-based nanomedicines in which carriers are conceived as inert vehicles of toxic drugs. Our findings enable the development of clever anticancer nanoplatforms that synergistically combine nanomedicines that perturb the mitochondrial electron transport chain (ROS production) with PDE-5 inhibitors which trigger oxidative stress specifically in cancer cells regardless of their location.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro cell models and culturing
Synthesis of the carbon nanodots-PEG4-β-cyclodextrin conjugate (CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx)
Characterization of CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx and precursor compounds
Drug loading and in vitro drug release kinetics
In vitro cytotoxicity assay on healthy, pre-cancerous and breast cancer cell models
In vitro cell uptake and intracellular fate studies of CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx
Reactive oxygen species (ROS) detection on cells treated with CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx@SD
Gene expression analysis by Real-Time quantitative polymerase chain reaction (qPCR)
The regioselective conjugation of β-cyclodextrin on carbon nanodots’ surface provides multicolor homogeneous ultra-small nanomedicines
The β-cyclodextrin conjugation allows keeping the fluorescence of CDs and provides controlled release of a high amount of sildenafil
The intracellular fate of the CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx conjugate depends on the cell line and implies different cytotoxic effects

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We would like to thank Advanced Technology Environment Network Center, Viale Delle Scienze Ed. 18, 90128 Palermo, Italy, for the support, which contributed to characterize the nanosystem developed using AFM and DSC/TGA analyses. N.M. conceived and supervised the project, supervised the chemical, physicochemical and biological experiments, data curation, performed AFM and DSC/TDA studies, wrote and revised the manuscript draft; R.C. and M.A.U. equally contributed performing biological studies and the physicochemical characterization, and writing the manuscript draft; S.C. performed the gene expression analysis and wrote the manuscript draft; G.G. found resources and revised the manuscript; A.N. performed and supervised the gene expression analysis study and wrote the manuscript draft; G.C. found resources and revised the manuscript. The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time due to technical or time limitations. However, they are available on request.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic pathway adopted for the CDs-PEG4-βCdx conjugate. CDs were coupled via amide bond using the amino end-group of NH2PEG4-CC chains and carboxyl groups of the CDs surface, then alkyne functions were exploited to link N3-βCdx by Cu (I)-catalyzed akyne-azide click cycloaddition.

Physicochemical characterization of the CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx conjugate and precursors. a Infrared (IR) spectra for bare CDs and the CDs-PEG4-CC sample obtained in KBr pellet within 4000 – 400 cm−1. b IR spectra of CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx in comparison with β-Cdx-N3 and CDs-PEG4-CC. c Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) of the CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx conjugate, CDs-PEG4-CC and β-Cdx-N3. d Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of the CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx conjugate and the related precursor compounds. e Non-contact mode atomic force microscopy (AFM) of the CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx conjugate. eI Schematic representation of the structure and the average size obtained the analysis of the heights from the AFM micrographs.

Optical characterization and drug release studies. a Absorption spectra obtained for a dispersion of CDs (dashed line), CDs-PEG4-CC (black line), and CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx (blue line) in ultrapure water; colour symbols indicate the excitation bands from the blue to the red region of the electromagnetic spectrum. b Emission spectra of CDs (dashed line), CDs-PEG4-CC (black line), and CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx (blue line) solutions registered at emission wavelength of 360 and 540 nm. c 3D emission spectrum for the CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx conjugate. d Drug release kinetic of the CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx@SD and diffusion profile of free sildenafil in 1% Tween 80 containing PBS solution pH 7.4 at 37 °C. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Cell uptake study by fluorescence on human breast cancer (MCF-7), human bronchial pre-cancerous (16-HBE) and human healthy dermal fibroblast (NHDF) cell lines treated with self-fluorescent multicolour CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx (0.15 mg mL−1) for 24 h at 37 °C: blue, green and red channels are due to the multicolour fluorescence of CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx. a-aII Fluorescence imaging micrographs of MCF-7 cells. aIII Merge of all fluorescence channels for MCF-7. aIV Brightfield optical micrograph of MCF-7 cells. aV Merge. b-bII Fluorescence imaging micrographs of NHDF cells. bIII Merge of all fluorescence channels for NHDF. bIV Brightfield optical micrograph of NHDF cells. bV Merge. c-cII Fluorescence imaging micrographs of 16-HBE cells. cIII Merge of all fluorescence channels for 16-HBE. cIV Brightfield optical micrograph of 16-HBE cells. cV Merge. Magnification 100x. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Cytotoxicity studies on human breast cancer, pre-cancerous human bronchial and healthy human fibroblasts cell lines. a-a’ Cell viability for the bare CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx conjugate after 24 h and 48 h exposure, respectively. b-b’ Cell viability for the free SD and equivalent amount of CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx@SD after 24 h and 48 h exposure, respectively. c-c’ Relative amount of ROS produced by cells treated with CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx@SD and parent compounds at the IC50 after 24 and 48 h of incubation. Values are expressed as means and standard errors of the mean (s.e.m.) with n = 3. Differences were considered statistically significant at p < 0.05 (*), p < 0.01 (**), and p < 0.001 (***).

Gene expression analysis of the oxidative stress pathways on the breast cancer cell line MCF-7. Heat map representation of the mean-centered data of RT-qPCR results showing the mRNA levels of analyzed genes in presence of CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx, SD, or CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx@SD at 24 and 48 h with respect to GAPDH, and ACTB. Gene expression values are colored from red (low) to green (high). Fold-changes in log (2)-transformed values are represented. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Half maximal inhibitory concentration values after 24 h (IC50 24h) and 48 h (IC50 48h) of incubation. All values were calculated by plotting the dose–response fitting considering the experimental points and performing an interpolation for y = 50 % (Origin Lab Software 8.5, r2 > 0.993).

head.ce:title: Controlled delivery of sildenafil by β-Cyclodextrin-decorated sulfur-doped carbon nanodots: a synergistic activation of ROS signaling in tumors overexpressing PDE-5

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent sulfur- and nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (CDs) are zero-dimensional nanoparticles that mediate ROS production in cancer cells, displaying inherent anticancer properties. Thus, they have been proposed as nanotheranostic tools useful in image-guided cancer therapy. Here, we try to show that cancerous cells (high PDE-5 expression) receiving sildenafil delivered by CDs-based nanostructures promote positive reinforcement of PDE-5-mediated cell death via the overexpression of genes involved in the production of ROS.

We explored the regioselective Huisgen cycloaddition between azide-β-cyclodextrin and CDs-alkyne to synthetize homogeneous nanostructures, named CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx, consisting of CDs functionalized at the surface with β-cyclodextrins capable of including high amount drugs such as sildenafil (>20 % w/w), and releasing them in a controlled manner. We investigated how CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx bearing sildenafil enter cells, enhancing ROS production and cell death specifically in cancer cells overexpressing PDE-5. These nanoplatforms go beyond the bounds of EPR-based nanomedicines in which carriers are conceived as inert vehicles of toxic drugs. Our findings enable the development of clever anticancer nanoplatforms that synergistically combine nanomedicines that perturb the mitochondrial electron transport chain (ROS production) with PDE-5 inhibitors which trigger oxidative stress specifically in cancer cells regardless of their location.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, there has been increasing interest in formulating active compounds within nanosystems, which offers a promising approach to enhance the effectiveness of anticancer drugs while minimizing their side effects (Ghosh and Biswas, 2021; Mauro et al., 2021b, 2021a, 2016; Singh et al., 2016). Nanomedicines are envisioned as inert vehicles capable of delivering cytotoxic drugs to the tumor site, utilizing active targeting through the recognition of receptors overexpressed on cancer cell membranes and/or passive targeting exploiting the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect (Islam et al., 2021; Kang et al., 2020). However, achieving selective accumulation of nanomedicines at the tumor site to reduce side effects and maximize anticancer efficacy remains challenging, since the existence of the EPR effect in humans has not been observed (Danhier, 2016) (Mauro et al., 2021b; Ramachandra Kurup Sasikala et al., 2016).

Therefore, regardless the EPR effect, it is essential to develop strategies that can mitigate severe side effects, prevent iatrogenic diseases, and improve the effectiveness of nanomedicines. Among available strategies, the use of ultrasmall multi-responsive nanocarriers (5–20 nm in diameters) able to diffuse throughout the body that combine chemotherapy with controlled physical/chemical insults (e.g., phototherapy and mitochondria targeting) has been proposed as a promising way to overcome these limitations (Fiorica et al., 2017; Li et al., 2017; Mauro et al., 2022, 2021b, 2021a, 2020b, 2020a, 2015; Ramachandra Kurup Sasikala et al., 2016; Scialabba et al., 2019). Ultrasmall nanocarriers endowed with remotely-controlled (e.g., photothermally, magnetically, photoacoustically) inherent and selective anticancer properties are desirable on this ground. These allow efficient distribution inside the tumor parenchyma owing to the high diffusion in collagen-rich tumor microenvironment (TME), as well as in metastatic niches, and provide suitable and localized damages once triggered on demand (Boakye-Yiadom et al., 2019; Mauro et al., 2022; Mishra et al., 2018). They can effectively target and reach cancer cells, bypassing the need for reliance on the EPR effect, thus offering an alternative approach for precise drug delivery in cancer therapy. Along the same line, it is possible to employ nanosystems with intrinsic biological activity, which can be effectively enhanced through the co-delivery of drugs possessing a safe pharmacological profile and exerting preferential action at the tumor site.

The interaction of some nanocarrier with biological systems can induce oxidative stress, leading to potential cellular damage. The generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) by nanoparticles can disrupt cellular redox balance and trigger oxidative stress pathways (del Rosal et al., 2018; Mauro et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2020). Paradoxically, this oxidative stress can be harnessed for therapeutic purposes in cancer treatment. For instance, certain nanoparticles, such as metal-based nanoparticles (i.e., gold nanoparticles, superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles) and carbon nanomaterials (i.e., carbon nanotubes, carbon nanodots), can induce ROS-mediated cytotoxicity preferentially in cancer cells, while sparing normal cells. This targeted oxidative stress approach holds promise for developing innovative and more effective cancer therapies. Among these, sulfur-doped carbon nanodots (CDs), with a 4–10 nm carbonaceous core occasionally doped with sulfur and nitrogen atoms and functionalized with polar surface groups, exhibit notable intrinsic anticancer effects (Ji et al., 2020b; Li et al., 2020; Mauro et al., 2022). Accumulating evidence suggests that these CDs possess exceptional ROS generation capabilities in cancer cells compared to normal cells. At least in principle, the release of ROS induced by CDs can be amplified by well-known and safe drugs that target the ROS pathway, thereby resulting in synergistic and more selective antitumor effects. For instance, by utilizing drugs that promote ROS production through their interaction with overexpressed targets in tumor cells (e.g., PDE-5 receptors), a selective increase in ROS levels by the coexisting CDs in tumor cells could be achieved. This strategy would offer several obvious benefits independent of the EPR effect and active targeting strategies: i) avoiding side effects on tissues lacking an adequate number of drug-activatable receptors, ii) inducing tumor cell death, and iii) targeting and eliminating tumor cells present in the circulatory system or potentially residing within pre-metastatic niches. In accordance with these principles, sildenafil (SD) has been showed to increase the sensitivity of many cancer cells overexpressing PDE-5 to cytotoxic effects of chemotherapeutic agents, enhancing ROS generation, increasing apoptosis mediated by means of the downregulation of genes involved in programmed cell death homeostasis (Bcl-xL, FAP-1, Bcl-2 BAD) and inhibiting the efflux activity of ATP-binding cassette (ABC) transporters (Bermejo et al., 2018; Francis et al., 2011; Haider et al., 2021a; Rotella, 2002). Noteworthy is that because of their smooth muscle relaxation capabilities sildenafil has been reported as a novel drug enhancing the delivery of nanomedicines to tumors in mice, suggesting a possible role in enhancing EPR in humans by restoring tumor blood circulation and lymphatic drainage (Islam et al., 2021; Yuan et al., 1993).

In this study, we sought to investigate for the first time whether the delivery of sildenafil using CDs capable of targeting cancer cells overexpressing PDE-5 provides a selective ROS generation and synergistic cancer cell death. We synthetized S- and N-doped CDs showing potential ROS-mediated anticancer effects in order to conjugate them with β-cyclodextrin surface moieties, suitable to bind drugs such as sildenafil citrate and release them in a controlled manner. The β-cyclodextrin surface moieties exhibit great promise for optimizing the therapeutic potential of sildenafil. With its unique structural characteristics, including a hydrophobic cavity and a hydrophilic shell, β-cyclodextrin has the ability to form inclusion complexes with sildenafil, resulting in enhanced solubility and improved stability (Al Omari et al., 2006). Furthermore, the high biocompatibility, coupled with its capacity to modulate drug release kinetics, makes it an enticing candidate for releasing the drug payload in close proximity to the CDs core in cancer cells.

We developed an innovative protocol to obtain β-cyclodextrin-CDs conjugates with a well-organized structure and narrow size-distribution, which relies on the azide-alkyne click chemistry approach, desired features for pharmaceutical applications. Finally, we investigated how this conjugate interact with healthy and cancer cell lines expressing different amount of PDE-5 as well as the impact on cell viability, the production of oxidative stress, and the expression of genes involved in the ROS homeostasis. The aim of this study is to attempt to demonstrate whether the synthesis of a ultrasmall and well-organized β-cyclodextrin-CDs conjugate enables controlled delivery of sildenafil, resulting in synergistic cancer cells death via the selective generation of ROS in PDE-5-overexpressing cancer cells.

Urea (99%), citric acid (99.5%), amino-PEG4-alkyne, p-toluenesulfonyl chloride, 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC, 99.5 %), N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 99.5%), L-ascorbic acid (99%), copper (II) sulfate (99.5 %), 1,1,2,2-tetrachloroethane (99.9 %), sildenafil citrate (SD, pharmaceutical secondary standard), SpectraPor® Float-A-Lyzer®G2 (MWCO 100–500 Da), SpectraPor® Pre-wetted RC Tubing (MWCO 1 kDa), Cation exchange resin Dowex® (50 W × 8 100–200 mesh), phosphate buffered saline (PBS) and Dulbecco’s phosphate-buffered saline (DPBS) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (Milan, Italy). β-cyclodextrin (99%) and sodium azide (NaN3 99.5%) were purchased from Fisher Scientific (Milan, Italy). Dulbecco's Minimum Essential Medium (DMEM), fetal bovine serum (FBS), L-glutamine, penicillin, streptomycin and amphotericin B were purchased from EuroClone (Milan, Italy). CellTiter 96® AQueous One Solution Cell Proliferation Assay (MTS) was purchased from Promega (Milan, Italy).
The Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) Detection Assay Kit was purchased from abcam plc. and used following the supplier’s instructions for adherent cells.
S, N- doped carbon nanodots (CDs) of 5.3 ± 0.4 nm diameter were obtained as previously reported, and their characterization is reported in supplementary (Fig. S1-S2) (Mauro et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cell models and culturing
ce:section.ce:para: The human bronchial epithelial cell line 16-HBE, derived from normal human bronchial cells immortalized with the SV-40 large T-antigen gene, was employed as well differentiated non-invasive stage of lung tumor progression overexpressing PDE-5 (A1 and A2 isoforms) (Cozens et al., 1994; Lin et al., 2006; Martinella-Catussel et al., 1999) The ER+ breast cancer cell line MCF-7 was used as a model of cancer overexpressing moderate amount of PDE-5 (A2 isoform) (Lin et al., 2006). While, primary normal human dermal fibroblasts (NHDF) areisolated from the dermis of juvenile foreskin, and were selected as a model of cell line without dysfunctions due to the overexpression of PDE-5(Lin et al., 2006).

All cell lines employed in this study were cultured in Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM), supplemented with FBS (10 % v/v), 2 mM L-glutamine, penicillin G (100 U mL−1), streptomycin (100 μg mL−1), amphotericin B (2.5 μg mL−1), at 37 °C and 5 % CO2. In particular, each cell line was seeded on a 96 wells-plate with a cell density of 1.5 × 104 for well and cultured in DMEM for 24 h before incubating them with samples.

β-cyclodextrins (β-Cdx) (10 g, 8.8 mmol) were dispersed in 90 mL of ultrapure water, and 2.5 M NaOH (35.2 mL, 88 mmol) was slowly added under magnetic stirring. After complete dispersion of β-Cdx, p-toluenesulfonyl chloride (2.52 mg, 13.22 mmol) was added in five portions and the suspension was stirred for 1 h at room temperature. The product was filtered, and washed with ultrapure water (10 mL). The filtered solution was added to 91 mL of pre-swollen cation exchange resin (Dowex®, 50 W × 8 100–200 mesh) and kept under stirring for 20 min. Then, the exchange resin was removed by vacuum filtration through a Büchner funnel with glass wool and washed in turn with water and acetone. The white powder (β-Cdx-OTs) was dried under vacuum (Yield: 31 %).

β-Cdx-OTs (2.5 mg, 1.95 mmol) was then dispersed in water (250 mL), and sodium azide (2.5 mg, 38.45 mmol) was added under stirring. The reaction was kept at 100 °C under reflux overnight. After that, it was cooled down to room temperature and the mixture was filtered through a paper filter. The filtrate was concentrated to about 15 mL under reduced pressure and 1,1,2,2-tetrachloroethane (3 mL) was added dropwise to the concentrated filtrate. The resulting emulsion was maintained under gentile stirring for 30 min and the aqueous phase was removed by centrifugation (3000 rpm, 5 min). The organic solvent was distilled under reduced pressure, leaving a solid residue that was crystalized twice in hot water to give rise a white solid by centrifuging (3000 rpm, 5 min). The white solid (β-Cdx-N3) was dried under vacuum overnight (Yield: 40%).

β-Cdx-OTs: 1H NMR (DMSO‑d 6, 300.12 MHz): δH 7.74–7.72 (2H, H8 Ph), δ 7.43–7.40 (2H, H7 Ph), δ5.75 (14H, OH-2/OH-3), δ 4.83–4.76 (7H, H1 Cdx), δ 4.33–4.29 (2H, H’6 Cdx, overlap with water), δ 4.18 (1H, H’5 Cdx, overlap with water), δ 3.31 (14H, H2/H4 Cdx, overlap with water), δ 2.14 (3H, Ph-CH3 ) ppm.
β-Cdx-N3: 1H NMR (DMSO‑d 6, 300.12 MHz): δH 6.90 (C2H2Cl4 trace), δ 5.78–5.65 (14H, OH-2/OH-3 Cdx), δ 4.82 (7H, H1 Cdx), δ 4.57 (6H, OH-6 Cdx), δ 3.33–3.30 (14H, H2/H4 Cdx, overlap with water).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the carbon nanodots-PEG4-β-cyclodextrin conjugate (CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx)
ce:section.ce:para: In the first step, CDs (20 mg, 2.86 mg mL−1) were dispersed in PBS pH 6.4 and amino-PEG4-alkyne (CC) (25 mg) were added. The pH was adjusted to 6.4 and EDC (24.92 mg, 0.13 mmol) and NHS (14.96 mg, 0.13 mmol) were added at onece under stirring. The reaction was kept at pH 6.4 for 24 h and purified by SpectraPor® Float-A-Lyzer®G2 (MWCO 100–500 Da) for 36 h. The product was then freeze dried to get a dark powder of CDs-PEG4-CC (yield: 77.3 %).

CDs-PEG-β-Cdx were obtained by the click cycloaddition of a dispersion of CDs-PEG4-CC (10 mg) and β-Cdx-N3 (20 mg) in ultrapure water (6 mL). Copper (II) sulfate (4 mg- 10 % cat.) and ascorbic acid (15 mg- 10 % cat.) were dispersed in 0.5 mL of ultrapure water, and added to the reaction mixture as catalyst. The reaction was kept under nitrogen atmosphere and magnetic stirring overnight. Then, cation exchange resin (Dowex®, 50 W × 8 100–200 mesh) was added to the mixture and, after 10 min of stirring, the resin was eliminated by filtration through a Büchner funnel with glass wool. The filtrate product was purified by gel permeation chromatography using a Sephadex G15. Fractions obtained were freeze dried obtaining a violet powder (CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx) (Yield: 91 %).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx and precursor compounds
ce:section.ce:para: The surface functional groups of CDs-PEG4-CC and bare CDs were investigated by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) in the range of 4000–400 cm−1. Samples were prepared as KBr pellet and dried in vacuum. Measurements were performed using a PerkinElmer Spectrum Two™ IR spectrometer (Waltham, MA, USA).

The size distribution of CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx was evaluated on the basis of heights from atomic force microscopy (AFM) micrographs. Samples were prepared by deposition of an aqueous dispersion of CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx (10 µL, 0.1 mg mL−1) on a mica substrate, that was dried under vacuum (10 mbar). AFM measurements were carried out in air using a Bruker FAST-SCAN microscope equipped with a closed-loop scanner (X, Y, Z maximum scan region: 35 µm, 35 µm, 3 µm, respectively). Scans were obtained in soft tapping mode using a FAST-SCAN-A probe with apical radius of about 5 nm and each image was obtained with a resolution comparable to the tip radius. The conjugation between CDs-PEG4-CC and β-Cdx-N3 were assessed by DSC/TGA analysis, using a DSC/TGA 131 EVO (by SETARAM Instr.). Analyses were performed using about 4 mg of dried powder of either CDs-PEG4-CC, β-Cdx-N3 or CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx placed into an aluminum crucible under nitrogen atmosphere (N2 flow: 1 mL min−1, heating rate: 10 °C min−1, temperature range: 30 – 500 °C).

Optical absorption and emission properties of CDs, CDs-PEG4-CC and CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx were evaluated on aqueous dispersions (0.25 mg mL−1). The absorption spectra were carried out by using a double-beam spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-2401PC) recording the spectra within the range 200–800 nm. The emission spectra under excitation ranging from 360 to 540 nm were recorded by a spectrofluorometer (Jasco FP-8500) with a resolution of 1 nm.
The structural characterization of the CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx conjugate was performed by 1H NMR in DMSO‑d 6 (10 mg mL−1) using a Bruker Avance II 300 spectrometer operating at 300.12 MHz.

CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx@SC were prepared via the kneading method. Dried powders of CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx (20 mg) and sildenafil citrate (SC, 5 mg) were pounded in a mortar until a homogeneous mixture was obtained. The powder were then kneaded adding a mixture of ethanol/water 70:30 (3 mL) dropwise, obtaining a thin film after the complete evaporation of solvents. Then, the film was suspended in ultrapure water (4 mL) by kneading. The suspension was stirred for 1 h, and subsequently dialysed for 1 h against water using a SpectraPor® Float-A-Lyzer®G2 (MWCO 500 Da). The product was retrieved as brown powder via freeze-drying (Yield: 89 %).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading and in vitro drug release kinetics
ce:section.ce:para: The amount of SD loaded into CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx was determined by HPLC analysis modifying a method reported elsewhere (Ghasemian et al., 2013a). In particular, CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx@SC were dissolved in the eluent (acetonitrile / water pH = 4 35:65) by sonicating it for 2 h. After that, samples were filtered by means of a 0.45 μm syringe filter, and the analysis was performed using an Agilent 1260 infinity HPLC System equipped with a C6-Phenyl column (Phenomenex®) under isocratic conditions (flow rate 0,8 mL min−1, temperature 25 °C, UV detector λ = 291 nm). The retention time was 4,8 min. To evaluate the amount of SD, the peak area of each sample was compared to a calibration curve obtained using SC standard solutions ranging from 0,1 to 0,005 mg mL−1 (y = 28,705x, R2 = 0.999). (Drug loading, DL = 19.9 % w w-1).

For the evaluation of drug release kinetics, either free SC or CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx@SC, in such quantity as to obtain an equivalent drug concentration (0.19 mg/mL), were dissolved in 1% Tween 80-containing PBS pH 7.4. Solutions were placed into a dialysis tubing of 1 kDa MWCO against 10 mL of the same medium. At scheduled time intervals (1 h, 2 h, 4 h, 6 h, 8 h, 24 h and 48 h), the external medium (0.5 mL) was withdrawn and replaced with fresh medium. The amount of SC was then determined by HPLC, using the method above described for the drug loading. The cumulative release of SD was plotted over time (s.e.m. n = 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity assay on healthy, pre-cancerous and breast cancer cell models
ce:section.ce:para: The cytocompatibility of CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx was assessed in vitro on NHDF, 16-HBE and MCF-7 cell lines. Each cell line was seeded on a 96 wells-plate with a cell density of 1.5 × 104 per well, and cultured for 24 h in DMEM at 37 °C and 5 % CO2. After that, culture medium was replaced with a dispersion of CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx in fresh medium (200 µL) at different concentration (10–––250 μg mL−1), and cells were incubated for 24 h or 48 h. The dispersion was then removed and each well was washed three times with DPBS at pH 7.4 before performing the cell viability assay. Cell viability was evaluated by the cell proliferation MTS assay, incubating each well with fresh DMEM (100 µL) and the MTS solution (20 µL) for about 2 h. Cell viability was obtained by measuring the absorbance at 492 nm using a microplate reader (Multiskan, Thermo, U.K.), and employing untreated cells as control. Data were reported in terms of percentage cell viability against control (viability 100 %) as a function of the CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx concentration.

In another set of experiments, the anticancer effect of CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx@SD was established using the same MTS protocol and in vitro cell models above described. Comparable amounts of free SD (0.5 – 50 μg mL−1) were used as positive control. All biological experiments were performed in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cell uptake and intracellular fate studies of CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx capability of entering different kind of cells reaching nuclei was studied in vitro on NHDF, 16-HBE and MCF-7 cell lines. In detail, cells were seeded on a 8 wells-chambered coverglass at a cell density of 5 × 103 per well, and cultured in DMEM for 24 h. Successively, the medium was replaced with 200 µL of CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx dispersion in DMEM (150 μg mL−1). After 24 h of incubation, the medium was removed and each well was washed three times with DPBS. Cell uptake micrographs were recorded using a fluorescence microscope Axio Cam MRm (Zeiss) exploiting the multicolor emission of CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx (blue, green and red emissions are due to self-fluorescence of the conjugate).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) detection on cells treated with CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx@SD
ce:section.ce:para: The release of ROS due to the exposure to CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx@SD and parent compounds at the IC50 was assessed in vitro on NHDF, 16-HBE and MCF-7 cell lines. Each cell line was seeded on a 96 wells-plate with a cell density of 2.5 × 104 per well, and cultured for 24 h in DMEM at 37 °C and 5 % CO2 to obtain about 80 % confluency on the3 day of experiment. Cells were than incubated for 24 h or 48 h with either CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx@SD, free SD or CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx. After that, the medium was removed, cells were treated with the ROS Assay Buffer I/ROS Label following the supplier’s instructions (Reactive Oxygen Species - ROS - Detection Assay Kit by abcam plc.). Thus, cells were washed up with DPBS pH 7.4 (200 μL) and treated with ROS Assay Buffer I/ROS Assay Buffer (100 μL) to measure fluorescence immediately by a multiplate reader (Ex/Em = 495/529 nm, Multiskan, Thermo, U.K.). Untreated cells were used as negative control, while cells treated with tert-Butyl hydroperoxide (100 μL, 1 μM) were used as positive control. Self-fluorescence of cells treated with the nanosystem alone was measured and subtracted from the measures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gene expression analysis by Real-Time quantitative polymerase chain reaction (qPCR)
ce:section.ce:para: Total RNA was extracted from control and treated cells (about 5000 cells per sample) using TRIzol Reagent (Invitrogen Corporation, Carlsbad, CA, USA) following the manufacturer’s instructions and the RNA was stored at − 80 °C. Purified RNA was treated with DNase I, Amplification Grade (Sigma-Aldrich, Milan, Italy) at 37 °C for 30 min to remove any genomic DNA contamination. After that, the DNase I was inactivated by adding 50 mM EDTA. First-strand cDNA was synthesized from DNase I-treated RNA samples using the High-Capacity cDNA Reverse Transcription Kit (Thermo Fischer Scientific, Carlsbad, CA, USA) following the manufacturer’s instructions. The cDNA mixture was tested by PCR using GAPDH, and ACTB primers (Table SX) and diluted 1:5 before use in Real-Time qPCR experiments.

qPCRs analyses were performed using the BIO-RAD CFX96 system with BlasTaq 2X qPCR MasterMix (Applied Biological Materials Inc, Richmond, BC, Canada) as detection chemistry. ACTB and GAPDH genes were selected as control genes based on their expression stability in all tested conditions. A normalization factor was calculated based on geometric averaging of ACTB, and GAPDH expression and was used to quantify the mRNA levels of the target genes (Vandesompele et al., 2002). Serial dilutions of pooled cDNAs from both control and treated samples were prepared to determine the PCR efficiency of the target and reference genes (data not shown), with amplification efficiency ranging from 1.9 to 2.1. Primer sequences used in this study are listed in Table S1.

qPCRs were conducted according to the manufacturer’s recommended procedures, and each reaction was repeated in triplicate. The amplification conditions were the following: initial denaturation at 95 °C for 3 min and 40 cycles of 95 °C for 15 s and 60 °C for 60 s, followed by a melting curve from 60 °C to 95 °C. Amplicons were detected by agarose gel analysis after each PCR to confirm the amplification of the specific gene product.
Gene expression results are presented as heat maps mRNA levels are represented as mean centered; while S.D. (n = 3) were below 0.5%. The graphs were generated via R software v. 3.5.0.

Statistical analysis was performed by the two-tailed t-test in OriginPro 8.5 software package. Comparisons were considered statistically significant at p < 0.05 (*), p < 0.01 (**), and p < 0.001 (***).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The regioselective conjugation of β-cyclodextrin on carbon nanodots’ surface provides multicolor homogeneous ultra-small nanomedicines
ce:section.ce:para: It has been recently shown that sulfur- and nitrogen- co-doped carbon nanodots (S/N-CDs) possess multicolor emission, suitable for fluorescence imaging applications, coupled with the ability of stimulating moderate ROS production in cancer cells leading to cell death (Ji et al., 2020b; Mauro et al., 2022; Miao et al., 2017). In particular, we previously showed that S/N-CDs obtained by solvothermal decomposition of citric acid, urea and small amount of indocyanine green, used as doping agent, provoke a selective cytotoxic effect in breast cancer cells instead of healthy ones, which can be exploited to produce theranostic agents for precision cancer medicine approaches (Mauro et al., 2022). Here, we attempted to enhance the inherent ROS-mediated anticancer effect of these S/N-CDs, henceforth named CDs, with the recently observed induction of ROS generation by means of sildenafil, a potent inhibitor of phosphodiesterase 5 (PDE-5) that has also showed inhibitory effects on the Bcl-2, Bcl-xL, and efflux ATP-binding cassette (ABC) transporters (Haider et al., 2021b; Mei et al., 2015).

In a second step, 6-monoazide-β-cyclodextrin (β-Cdx-N3) was prepared by in turn mono-tosylation of the hydroxyl group in position 6, leading to the intermediate 6-O-monotosyl-β-Cdx, and subsequent nucleophilic substitution of the tosyl leaving group with the azide function (Fig. S3-S4) (Tripodo et al., 2013). The efficient nucleophilic substitution by means of azide ions to give 6-monoazide-β-cyclodextrin (β-Cdx-N3) was reached under mild and green conditions using boiling water as solvent.

Finally, CDs-PEG4-CC were conjugated with the β-Cdx-N3 by the regioselective copper-catalyzed Huisgen cycloaddition between the alkyne functions and the azide group. In principle, each CDs-PEG4-CC nanoparticle contains many alkynes surface groups, whereas the β-Cdx-N3 bears only one azide group which can react with only one dot, thus avoiding dot-to-dot crosslinking and providing homogeneous functionalized samples (Fig. 1). The feasibility of this reaction was demonstrated by the FTIR spectra reported in Fig. 2b, where typical vibrations of the β-Cdx groups can be distinguished in the spectrum of the CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx conjugate at 1000–1300 cm−1 (C-O-C stretching) and 2950 cm−1 (C–H stretching), coupled with the characteristic vibrations of the CDs-PEG4 core at 2870 cm−1 (C–H stretching) and 1550–1720 cm−1 (CO stretching and I/II amide bands). Besides, the lack of the distinctive stretching of the β-Cdx-N3 azide group at 2100 cm−1 is a clear prove that β-Cdx groups are linked by 1–3 triazole functions arisen from the azide-alkyne click reaction (Fig. 2b).

The chemical composition and the hypothesized structure were also confirmed by differential scanning calorimetry (Fig. 2c) and thermogravimetry (Fig. 2d). In particular, Fig. 2c shows that the CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx thermogram is complex and consists of typical exothermic transitions observed for the CDs-PEG4-CC (130 °C) and the bare -β-Cdx-N3 (230 and 270 °C). However, these transitions appear broader and slightly shifted at higher temperatures for the -β-Cdx portion (240 and 286 °C). As reported in Fig. 2d, these transitions are due to a weight loss of about 1.8 % w/w and 52 % w/w owing to eecomposition phenomena of the CDs core and -β-Cdx chains, respectively. The higher thermal stability observed for the CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx sample implies an effective covalent conjugation between CDs-PEG4 and the β-Cdx moieties. The enthalpy values of the peaks at 240 and 286 °C were compared with those of the bare β-Cdx-N3 in order to estimate the number of β-Cdx moieties linked at the CDs surface. We calculated that there are about seven β-Cdx moieties per dot, corresponding to roughly 35 % on a weight basis.

The size distribution of the CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx conjugate was evaluated by atomic force microscopy (AFM), considering the heights of all objects in a field of about 25 μm2. As reported in Fig. 2e the sample is highly homogeneous, showing dots of about 8.1 ± 0.2 nm in diameter without hinting at dot-to-dot crosslinking. According to the length calculated for a PEG chain of 400 Da functionalized with β-Cdx (about 1.5 nm), a characteristic CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx dot can be described as shown in Fig. 2e’, which consists of a CDs core of 5 nm passivated with PEG4-β-Cdx chains to give rise to well-organized nanoparticles of 8 nm carrying a CDs core and β-Cdx pendants. The regioselective azide-alkyne click conjugation between CDs-PEG4-CC and β-Cdx-N3 allows obtaining ultrasmall multifunctional nanomedicines suitable to load drugs and release them in a controlled fashion inside tumors. In fact, ultrasmall nanoparticles with diameter higher than the renal cut off (5 nm) are required to promote a rapid diffusion throughout the tumor microenvironment (TME) and into the tumor parenchyma, thus allowing an efficient accumulation of actives in the site of action (Ji et al., 2020a; Kim et al., 2018; Mauro et al., 2021b, 2021a, 2020b, 2020a; Scialabba et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The β-cyclodextrin conjugation allows keeping the fluorescence of CDs and provides controlled release of a high amount of sildenafil
ce:section.ce:para: The optical absorption spectrum of CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx is reported in Fig. 3 a by comparison with the bare CDs and the CDs-PEG4-CC intermediate, and shows a sharp UV absorption below 300 nm probably due to CDs core states followed by a series of electronic transitions up to 750 nm, with main peaks at 350, 440, 560, and 650 nm. These transitions can be associated with well-established surface states studied elsewhere (Messina et al., 2016; Scialabba et al., 2019; Sciortino et al., 2018). The same behavior is observed for the bare CDs and the CDs-PEG4-CC precursor, indicating that the surface passivation of starting CDs and the subsequent conjugation of PEG chains with β-Cdx moieties do not impinge on the electronic transitions of CDs. The intact fluoresce displayed by the conjugate implies that CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx can be tracked by fluorescence imaging from the blue to the red region, desired characteristics for in vivo and ex-vivo high-resolution imaging applications (Hilderbrand and Weissleder, 2010; Ko et al., 2013; Lee et al., 2013). Most importantly, surface conjugation with β-Cdx via 1–3-triazole preserves the extended optical absorption towards the red/NIR region, due to S-doped dots (Yang et al., 2019), thus allowing NIR photothermal applications (Fiorica et al., 2017; Mauro et al., 2017, 2015). The complex absorption bands lead to a wide tunability of the emission spectrum of the CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx conjugate, from 360 to 540 nm, according to the characteristic fluorescence of the bare CDs core (Fig. 3b). The fluorescence of CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx undergo a red shift about 40 nm (from 540 to 580 nm) with respect to the bare CDs and the parent PEGylated dots. This can be ascribed to electron effects owing to aromatic triazole groups at the CDs surface (Scialabba et al., 2019). As shown in Fig. 2c, an intense emission of CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx is observed from the blue to the red region of the visible spectrum, with quantum yield (QY) from 6.5 to 2.4 %, respectively.

The CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx conjugate was employed to load sildenafil citrate (SD), used as a model of drug which trigger ROS-induced damages in many tumors. We prepared SD/β-Cdx inclusion complexes, henceforth named CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx@SD, by using the kneading method and exploiting -β-Cdx moieties as host functions. In particular, an ethanol/water mixture was employed to form the film, which was finally dispersed in water and purified by filtration and dialysis against water. The drug loading calculated, established by HPLC analysis, was 20.6 % (Ghasemian et al., 2013b; Hosny et al., 2020; Paranjpe et al., 2013). We also assessed the ability of the CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx@SD of releasing the payload in a controlled way up to 48 h of incubation under physiological conditions. The drug release profile is displayed in Fig. 3d and show that about 55 % of the payload is release until the first 10 h from the incubation, significantly lower if compared with the diffusion curve of the free drug (87 % within 10 h). While, CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx@SD reaches a quasi-plateau after 10 h, releasing up to 70 % of the initial dose within 48 h. This trend implies that SD is reversibly loaded in the CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx@SD complex, thus providing a suitable release of sildenafil in the site of action after the administration. The binding constant (K 11) of SD/β-cyclodextrin complexes is relatively low (150 M−1), suggesting that the observed slow release can be attributed to the involvement of the nanostructure in the host's binding phenomena (Al Omari et al., 2006).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The intracellular fate of the CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx conjugate depends on the cell line and implies different cytotoxic effects
ce:section.ce:para: Sildenafil has been reported to increase the sensitivity of different tumor cells, enhancing the intracellular production of ROS and inducing the down regulation of many anti-apoptotic proteins (i.e., DAB, Bcl-2 and Bcl-xL) (Mei et al., 2015). In addition, it has been demonstrated that sildenafil can lead to augmentation of EPR due to the many effects showed on vasculature. Here, we assumed that sildenafil would be repurposed in precision anticancer applications since it might promote positive reinforcement of the ROS production due to nanomaterials endowed with inherent oxidative effects, such as S/N-doped CDs, coupled with a potential selective accumulation in solid tumors enhancing vasodilatation and, as consequence, the EPR effect in vivo. Thus, using the recent developed S/N-doped CDs (CDs) which showed the ability of specifically triggering ROS in cancer cells, we attempted to show that invasive breast cancer cell lines receiving sildenafil (in itself harmless) by the CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx@SD conjugate promote cell death. This concept potentially paves the way to a change in the nanomedicine paradigm, where a nanomedicine is considered only a vehicle for cytotoxic drugs, focusing the attention on the development of nanotechnologies with inherent biological effects that can be selectively triggered by the co-delivery of safe drugs such as sildenafil, which display synergistic effects.

With this in mind, we firstly studied the ability of the CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx conjugate of entering cancerous (MCF-7) and pre-cancerous (16-HBE) cells overexpressing PDE-5 receptors, or normal cells (NHDF) in vitro (Catalano et al., 2019; Cruz-Burgos et al., 2021; Klutzny et al., 2018).

We followed the bright multicolor fluorescence (blue, green and red) of the CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx up to 24 h from the incubation, evaluating their cell uptake and localization by fluorescence microscopy (Fig. 4 ). CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx efficiently enter all cell lines considered reaching all intracellular compartments after 24 h. However, the main cell localization observed in the breast cancer cell line MCF-7 is remarkably different if compared with the pre-cancerous and normal cell lines studied. In particular, it is evident a strong blue/green/red fluorescence inside MCF-7 nuclei which indicates a preferential accumulation of CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx in breast cancer cell nuclei (Fig. 4aI-aV). There is also a clear and a feeble blue fluorescence in endosomes and throughout the cytosol (Fig. 4aI), respectively, that is slightly visible in the green (Fig. 4aII) and red (Fig. 4aIII) channels, suggesting that CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx is also internalized through endocytosis and can somehow interact with cell vesicles significantly changing the QY from the blue to the red region. It seems that CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx can sense different intracellular compartments by coupling multicolor fluorescence imaging. On the contrary, except for the nuclei, CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx seem ubiquitously distributed in the cytosol of the normal cell line NHDF (Fig. 4 bI-bV). While, although CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx do not appear inside cell nuclei, for the pre-cancerous cell line 16-HBE similar sensing features can be seen when CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx are localized in vesicles and perinuclear regions (Fig. 4 cI-cV). On the whole, CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx can efficiently enter cancerous and pre-cancerous cells as well as the normal NHDF cell line, but they are localized in different cell districts which implies a preferential release of the sildenafil payload either in the nuclear, endosomes or cytosol compartments. This suggests that the predominant inhibition of PDE-5 in different compartments would provide various effects as a function of the cell type treated. It might be expected that the induction of apoptosis in cancer cells by restoring the NO/cGMP pathway and enhancing the production of ROS could be very different if compared with normal cells. Besides, induction of apoptosis in normal cells through PDE-5 inhibition requires concomitant activation of the cGMP-signaling pathway for instance by guanyl cyclase, which suggest a possible refractory behavior toward the sole PDE-5 inhibition (Supuran et al., 2006).

The possible role of sildenafil in locally triggering the CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx conjugate by enhancing the ROS pathway in cancerous and pre-cancerous cells, instead of healthy cells, was studied in vitro on MCF-7, NHDF and 16-HBE cell lines. In a first set of experiments, the cytocompatibility of CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx was evaluated until a concentration of 250 μg mL−1 after 24 (Fig. 5 a) or 48 h (Fig. 5 a’) of incubation with increasing amount of the conjugate. In Fig. 5 a-a’ it is clearly reported that for the healthy cell line NHDF CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx is cytocompatible within the entire range of concentration studied and for both incubation time. While, a feeble cytotoxic effect can be seen for pre-cancerous and cancerous cell lines at the highest concentration tested. In particular, cell viability reaches roughly 74 % and 68 % for 16-HBE and MCF-7, respectively. It seems that CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx displays inherent and selective cytotoxic insults toward cancer cells. These can be attributed to a selective ROS production in cancerous and pre-cancerous cells due to the S/N-doped CDs core. In fact, it has been reported that S/N-doped CDs induce mitochondria stress in cancer cells owing to sulfur doping, which implies a sharp production of ROS in vitro because of strong interactions with the electron transport chain in the mitochondrial membrane (Ji et al., 2020b; Zhang et al., 2020). When directly incubated with free sildenafil (up to 50 μg mL−1), we found that only 16-HBE cells undergo a significant reduction in cell viability at the higher concentration tested and only after 48 h from the incubation (Fig. 5 b-b’). This can be attributed to the higher overexpression of PDE-5, whose inhibition somehow intensify the cGMP-mediated production of ROS in cancer cells. Surprisingly, these cytotoxic effects drastically increase incubating cells with CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx@SD, where sildenafil is slowly released inside specific cell compartments (Fig. 3) depending on the kind of cell line studied. In particular, although combining sildenafil and the conjugate in CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx@SD we observed a slight increment of cytotoxicity for the healthy cell line NHDF (30 % VS 98 % cell death at 50 μg mL−1) (Fig. 5 b-b’), the cell viability of pre-cancerous (16-HBE) and cancerous (MCF-7) cell lines is about 0.5 % at the higher concentration tested after 24 h of incubation (Fig. 5b). However, the anticancer activity showed by CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx@SD is higher toward MCF-7, both after 24 and 48 h of incubation (Fig. 5 b-b’). A careful examination of the IC50 values reported in Table 1 reveals that the anticancer potency due to the combination of the intracellular delivery of sildenafil and the inherent CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx effects is two-fold bigger after 48 h of incubation, indicating a time-dependent efficacy, and about four-fold bigger for MCF-7 if compared with 16-HBE cells. While, no significant toxic insults can be seen in the healthy cell line NHDF. These results suggest that only cells who overexpress PDE-5 are liable to a helpful sildenafil-mediated activation of the CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx cytotoxic effects upon administration.

To further confirm the connectivity between sildenafil-mediated signaling and the enhancement of ROS production by means of CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx, we evaluated the total amount of ROS in cells treated at the IC50 with either free sildenafil, CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx or CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx @SD after 24 h and 48 h of incubation. According to literature data, being constituted by a sulfur-doped core, CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx induce a slight enhancement of the ROS production in cancer cells MCF-7 (Fig. 5 c-c’) (Ji et al., 2020b). While, the free drug can provoke an intensification of ROS levels both in MCF-7 and 16-HBE, with negligible effects on the healthy cell line studied (NHDF). These selective enhancement toward pre-cancerous and cancerous cell lines is exacerbated when sildenafil is delivered by the conjugate. Indeed, the amount of ROS produced by MCF-7 and 16-HBE incubated with CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx @SD at the IC50 is about 1–2-fold higher is compared with the levels measured in NHDF both after 24 and 48 h of incubation, and roughly 3–4-fold higher in comparison with cells treated with the free drug (Fig. 5 c-c’). These results, on the whole, confirm that the enhancement of ROS production specifically in pre-cancerous and cancerous cells depends on the concomitant sildenafil-mediated signaling, the overexpression of PDE-5 in cancer cells, and the peculiar intracellular trafficking of the nanosystem after administration. Besides, it is clear the outstanding role of sildenafil in triggering the inherent oxidative stress provided by the CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx conjugate via the production of intracellular ROS, thus causing precise and selective deleteriousness to cancer cell lines.

Next, we examined a pathway-focused gene expression analysis to evaluate the mRNA expression levels of 24 genes involved in the cellular oxidative stress signaling. In particular, we attempted to corroborate data on the sustained production of ROS in MCF-7 at the molecular level. We selected genes recognized to exert a functional role in ROS metabolism (ALOX12, NOS2, SOD1, SOD2, TXN, c), as antioxidants (CAT, GPX, GSR, GSTP1, PRDX1, PTGS2), as oxidative stress-responsive genes (AKR1C2, FTH1, GCLC, GCLM, HMOX1, NQO1, SQSTM1), and in the DNA damage and repair mechanisms (ATR, CHEK1, GADD45A, MRE11A, RAD51).

A comparison between MCF7 cells treated with CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx and unexposed MCF7 revealed a similar transcriptional profile either at 24 h and 48 h (Fig. 6 ). Of the 24 analyzed genes, only 2 showed upregulation higher than 2-fold in gene expression in CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx -treated cells at 24 h. While at 48 h the overall expression levels remained unchanged or slightly downregulated, thus confirming that CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx is cytocompatible and do not significantly impinge on the mitochondria homeostasis.

Similarly, cells exposed to sildenafil did not show substantial changes as among oxidative stress-responsive genes only AKR1C2 was upregulated. While, genes involved in ROS metabolism, in the antioxidant response and DNA damage, were unchanged or downregulated at 24 h. Conversely, mRNA levels of these genes resulted upregulated up to 4-fold compared to controls after 48 h exposure to sildenafil, thus confirming that effects exerted by sildenafil on ROS production are concentration- / exposure time-dependent (Fig. 6).

As expected, cells exposed to CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx@SD shows different gene expression patterns when compared with the control at 24 h. Among the gene set analyzed, we found at least 2- to 20-fold increased mRNA levels in 12 genes; in particular, it emerges a concurrent upregulation of all the ROS-responsive genes herein analyzed inn genes involved in ROS production and metabolism (ALOX12, NOS2, and TXNRD), in the antioxidant response (GPX, GSTP1, and PTGS2), and also in DNA repair (GADD45). Interestingly, the overall upregulation was boosted at 48 h since we found 10––250-fold increases in mRNA levels. Hence, considering the magnitude of the upregulations and the measured ROS amount it is reasonable to affirm that CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx@SD activates the oxidative stress pathway in exposed breast cancer cells.

Finally, we proved that the CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx conjugate is hemocompatibile up to 600 μg mL−1 since provokes the same damages observed for erythrocytes incubated in pristine DPBS (Fig. S5, erythrolysis < 3 %), demonstrating the feasibility of multiple parenteral administrations. This behavior is not trivial and suggest that monofunctionalized β-Cdx moieties loss the typical hemolytic profile of unmodified β-Cdx (Li and Loh, 2008; Singh et al., 2016; Weisz et al., 1993).

Here, we successfully developed a protocol for the synthesis of ultrasmall sulfur- and nitrogen-doped CDs bearing β-cyclodextrin at the surface, referred to as CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx. We evaluated their potential as drug delivery vehicles and explored their intrinsic anticancer effects on cancerous and pre-cancerous cells. Through azide-alkyne Huisgen cycloaddition between azide-β-Cdx and CDs-PEG4-alkyne, we obtained well-organized ultrasmall nanostructures with an average diameter of 8 nm. CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx consist of a multicolor sulfur-doped CDs functionalized at the surface with β-cyclodextrin moieties capable of including drugs, such as sildenafil, and releasing them in a controlled manner. Our CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx conjugate achieved the highest reported of sildenafil loading capacity (20.6 % w/w) among nanomedicines elsewhere reported. We employed sildenafil for its inhibition of tumor cells that overexpress PDE-5, thereby enhancing the intrinsic abilities of CDs to induce ROS formation in tumor cells. For the first time, we proved that although the CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx conjugate alone exhibited preferential production of ROS in cancerous and pre-cancerous cells, due to interactions with mitochondria membranes, single treatments do not significantly impinge on cancer cell viability. However, delivering sildenafil by the CDs-PEG4-β-Cdx conjugate results in a strong enhancement of the production of ROS in cancer and pre-cancerous cells, followed by a concurrent upregulation of all ROS-responsive genes (ALOX12, NOS2, TXNRD, GPX, GSTP1, PTGS2), which depend on the electronic properties of sulfur-doped CDs core of the conjugate coupled with a peculiar localization and expression of the PDE-5 in cancer cells. Our findings, despite the preliminary nature, hold great promise for repurposing sildenafil in precision cancer therapy, combining intrinsically active nanosystems (ROS production) with the inhibition of PDE-5 in cancer cells (enhanced expression of nitric oxide synthase - NOS - enzymes). These results highlight the importance of engineering nanomedicines to target critical cellular mechanisms, such as the mitochondrial electron transport chain, to leverage their inherent pharmacological effects using safe drugs as enhancers. However, additional studies are required to comprehensively understand sildenafil mechanisms and pathways of action adopted in cancer cells.
Their preliminary nature, we aim to ensure that readers understand the significance of our contribution within the broader context of scientific exploration.
